10.1109/i2ct57861.2023.10126192.title: Survey on Smartphone Sensors and User Intent in Smartphone Usage
10.1109/i2ct57861.2023.10126192.abstract: Smartphone/Tablet users are approximately 3 million all over the world. It is likely to increase by several 100 million in the next few years. Around 40% of these users read online. Explicit means of feedback system is strongly based. It provides the most accuracy when rating an online learning application. Increase in the availability of content over the web and high user engagements, has led to the demand of the means that implicitly provide feedback. Implicit feedback relies on understanding the quality of the content based on the user activities performed over the web applications. Less accuracy is the limitation. It needs to stand with a support to provide as strong base as the explicit model does. Clipboard copy operations on the webpage provide an implicit insight to the user intentions. Screen activities like scrolling and pinch to zoom further can statistically be proven the positive indicators of user interest. Smartphone sensors like Gyroscope and Accelerometer silently sense human screen activities and mobile gestures. This review paper is based on the understanding of smartphone sensors and the inferences of user intent through it. The dig is based on various implicit indicators like mobile gestures, smartphone sensors and clipboard copy operations.

10.1145/3508072.3508169.title: An Empirical Investigation on the Factors Affecting Smartphone Addiction in Digital Era: Effect of Social Networking Services and Instant Messaging Applications Usage
10.1145/3508072.3508169.abstract: In today's digital era severe smartphone usage is increased by several smartphone applications such as social networking services and instant messaging applications. In this study, we argue that use of social networking services and instant messaging applications may cause an individual's inevitable dependence to the smartphone which consequently leads to addiction. Thus, the current research examines use of both social networking services and instant messaging applications as predictors of smartphone addiction with some other antecedents of social networking services and instant messaging applications usage. Results show that use of social networking services and use of instant messaging applications are important predictors of smartphone addiction. Findings also represent that use of social networking services applications is affected by social networking services network size and use of instant messaging applications is affected by instant messaging network size and the number of instant messaging applications actively being used on smartphone. Moreover, social anxiety and peer influence are examined as the moderating effects between relationships of usage of social networking services and instant messaging applications and smartphone addictions. Results of these tests show that social anxiety moderates the relationship between use of social networking services applications and smartphone addiction and peer influence moderates the relationship between use of instant messaging applications and smartphone addiction. This study has contributions to the addiction research, and also implications to governments and health institutions pertaining to the effect of smartphone addiction on society and individual's health.

10.1109/iuser.2014.7002686.title: A study of smartphone usage and barriers among the elderly
10.1109/iuser.2014.7002686.abstract: A wide range of smartphone applications aimed at encouraging healthy diets and active lifestyles have been developed in recent years. However, the use of these smartphone applications among the elderly population that should benefit the most from them remains low. This paper reports on the barriers to smartphone usage among the elderly. In-depth interviews were conducted with the elderly to gather information about their demographics, experiences and the perceived barriers to using smartphones. Twenty-one participants were interviewed using a semi-structured interview method. Results show that the elderly have difficulties in using smartphones due to multiple reasons such as financial limitations, vision impairments, and lack of interest and knowledge in using technological devices and their advanced functionalities.

10.1109/nitc.2018.8550081.title: Smartphone VPN App Usage and User Awareness Among Facebook Users
10.1109/nitc.2018.8550081.abstract: The information security threat landscape is evolving daily with attackers increasingly resorting to ‘social engineering’ tactics to obtain information about their victims. Such information could be even those published on platforms such as Facebook, which can provide deep insights into users’ activities, preferences, likes, dislikes and behavioural patterns. Information of such nature is of great value in today's technology driven society, which is why individuals and organisations are increasingly trying to gather and store them, with or without the consent of the end-user. A common attack vector used for such purposes are mobile apps, primarily due to the fact that smartphones are increasingly capturing data about their users. This research attempts to identify user awareness levels among Sri Lankan smartphone users, when the usage of VPN apps increased suddenly during the period in which social media were banned. Analysis of responses provided by 150 respondents show that 85% admitted to using VPN apps during this period, with only 21.74% of Non-VPN users being aware of the security risks associated with VPN apps.

10.1016/j.sbspro.2012.05.117.title: Twitter Usage Habits of Undergraduate Students
10.1016/j.sbspro.2012.05.117.abstract: Twitter has a potential to be used as a professional and social networking since individuals who have similar interests can meet on Twitter. Twitter is a multiplatform Web 2.0, part social networking - part microblogging tool, freely accessibly on the Web. The aim of this study was to investigate the usage habits of undergraduate students on Twitter. The study was conducted on ninety three volunteered undergraduate students. The data obtained by the survey was analyzed and commented upon using the SPSS program with the percentage, frequency, and mean statistical analysis techniques. As the results show, majority of the participants, spent most of their time on Twitter. Quotes, photos, videos, music, news, IT news and magazine news are the most commonly shared items on Twitter. However, educational materials were observed to be least frequently shared by the students. From this premise, we argue that investigating the most preferred usage habits of students on Twitter is an important and hot topic research. We believe that in future days more research will be focused on usage of Twitter in education due to its characteristics and student's interest in this social network. Therefore, this study could be considered as an important preliminary study in this field.

10.1109/iccsnt.2016.8070174.title: Smartphone usage pattern based on geographical location and addiction issue
10.1109/iccsnt.2016.8070174.abstract: The outcome of smartphone greatly changes the life of people. It is of importance to understand the usage pattern of varieties of applications in social life. Many researches have centered on this topic through statistics of the log files. However, most of them treat the location of the user as an element of the statistical result but not the cause for the usage pattern. In this paper, by analyzing the time and frequency of APP in different location, we find the location of the user is one of crucial factors for usage pattern. The migration takes up a great proportion to change the habit of users, implying that it is an effective cure for addicted people.

10.1016/j.sbspro.2015.04.785.title: The University Students’ Knowledge of Internet Applications and Usage Habits
10.1016/j.sbspro.2015.04.785.abstract: The internet has become an indispensible part of our modern life in accordance with the information technologies that has been growing rapidly. The limitations concerning time, place, equipment and cost have been eased recently. Therefore, the frequency, purpose and quality of internet use have been greatly improved and the number of internet users has been increased tremendously. With the rise of the mobile devices, especially the students have started to use the internet more actively. This study aims to examine the level of the university students’ knowledge of internet and online applications. A survey including questions about more than one online application was used to identify the knowledge of the students. The questionnaires were given to the students of a school which has five undergraduate programs and a statistics software was used to analyze the obtained data. According to the analyses done, the internet use rates and the applications they use were evaluated.

10.1016/j.sbspro.2011.11.174.title: Social network sites usage habits of undergraduate students: case study of Facebook
10.1016/j.sbspro.2011.11.174.abstract: The usage of social network sites is increasing daily. Facebook, which is a popular social network site, is one of the most commonly used social sharing sites today, having millions of users. The aim of this study was to investigate the Facebook usage of students and also to learn which Facebook tools the participants preferred. Eighty six volunteer undergraduate students participated in the present study. The data obtained by the survey was analyzed and commented upon using the SPSS program with the percentage, frequency, and mean statistical analysis techniques. As the results show, students spend a significant amount of their times using the Facebook. Participants use Messages, Chat, Friends, Links, News and Photos tools are the most commonly used tools of Facebook. In addition, Facebook provides individuals with a way of maintaining and strengthening social ties, which can be beneficial in both social and academic settings. Future studies should concentrate on integrating the Facebook into education and teaching, which is important in students’ everyday working lives.

10.1109/pimrc.2018.8581005.title: Synthetic Smartphone Data Usage Modeling Through User Clustering
10.1109/pimrc.2018.8581005.abstract: Mobile network usage models (along with system models) are useful tools for estimating the performance of future and current mobile communication systems. However current mobile network usage models lack either the granularity or flexibility required for assessing advanced concepts (such as cognitive radio and dynamic spectrum access) at academic research levels. Therefore, in this work, we propose a network usage model that is both granular (modeling at the individual device level) and flexible (allowing arbitrary combinations of empirically derived user types and various time scales). We derive the user types through time-series clustering of network usage patterns of around 400 diverse US-based smartphone users. Additionally, the model accounts for the bursty nature and self-similarity of such usage and we show these characteristics are preserved over several time scales.

10.1109/ngmast.2011.12.title: Diversity and End User Context in Smartphone Usage Sessions
10.1109/ngmast.2011.12.abstract: Mobile end user context has gained increasing attention in the mobile services industry. Context information is seen as an important component in developing new, more personalized, mobile services and applications. This paper studies the effect of end user context on smart phone usage sessions. Smart phone usage sessions are used to depict user behavior and usage habits of smart phone users on a high level. We have detected end user contexts, and extracted smart phone usage session information from handset-based data of 140 smart phone users. We first examine and describe usage sessions as such, and then in different end user contexts. According to our usage session analysis, smart phone usage is highly diversified across users. For example, the average number of sessions per day ranges from 3 to 46. Characteristics of smart phone usage sessions differ in different end user contexts. For example, an average session is 37 % longer in the Home-context than in the Office-context, but Office has 56 % more sessions per time unit than Home. The results imply that mobile services and applications need to adapt to user behavior in order to be personalized enough, and that context awareness is indeed a worthwhile step towards this.

10.1109/bigdata50022.2020.9378172.title: User authentication based on smartphone application usage patterns through learning classifier systems
10.1109/bigdata50022.2020.9378172.abstract: Smartphones have become more ubiquitous than ever. People are installing various applications on their smart-phone to fit into their lifestyle. Existing research shows that there are patterns within the ways people access those applications, whether it involves particular locations, particular ranges of time, or many other factors. In this research, through a collaboration with a commercial company, we collected usage data from a popular smartphone application that gives its users access to digital flyers information for shops and supermarkets throughout Japan. Our early experiments found that the pattern information contained inside the data could be used to authenticate users. In this research, we are proposing a behavioral authentication model implementing customized learning classifier systems to search through vast amount of possible patterns to authenticate users of the application. Our early findings for this ongoing research demonstrate that our model can feasibly be a good alternative for additional authentication factor to implicitly authenticate users beyond the initial registration.

10.1109/icdmw69685.2025.00290.title: User Ranking System Using Pareto Optimality and Usage Time Metrics
10.1109/icdmw69685.2025.00290.abstract: Understanding user behavior through application usage patterns offers valuable insights for tasks such as user profiling and identification. A key challenge in these domains lies in distinguishing users who exhibit ideal usage patterns from those who deviate. Although time-based metrics-such as the number of active weeks, inactive weeks, and total usage lifetime-are commonly employed for this purpose, each captures different aspects of behavior, limiting their effectiveness when used in isolation. To address this issue, we propose two novel composite metrics, engagement rate and chance rate, derived from fundamental temporal usage data. These metrics are employed within a Pareto optimality framework to construct a continuous user ranking that prioritizes individuals demonstrating more consistent and desirable usage behavior. To evaluate the proposed methodology, we collect a comprehensive set of interaction features, including visit frequency, session duration, number of pages visited, time spent per page, and user interactions with individual pages. We then compare users in the top and bottom quartiles of the generated ranking to assess behavioral distinctions. Using Cramér's V, we quantify the strength of association between each feature and user ranking. The results indicate that the ranking system effectively discriminates between user groups, identifying six features with at least moderate association strength. Furthermore, we demonstrate the applicability of the ranking by employing it to guide a custom clustering process. The resulting clusters are compared against those produced by the traditional k-means algorithm. Empirical results show that the custom clustering informed by our ranking outperforms the baseline, offering a more nuanced segmentation of user behavior.

10.1109/trustcom63139.2024.00365.title: Your Data is Leaking! An Empirical Study of User Habits during Smartphone Charging
10.1109/trustcom63139.2024.00365.abstract: Mobile devices especially smartphones have been widely adopted in people’s daily lives. Due to the convenience and computing capability, users are likely to store their private data on the phones, making them a target for cyber-criminals. With an increase of public charging stations, charging attacks have become a big threat on users’ private information. Juice filming charging (JFC) attack is a particular charging threat, which can infer users’ private information through automatically recording phone screen during the whole charging period. Due to the nature of monitoring phone screen, the actual effect of JFC attack depends heavily on users’ charging habits. However, this issue has not been widely investigated in the literature. Motivated by this, our work presents an empirical study to investigate how phone users may interact with their phones during the charging period. The study reveals that up to 97.1% participants would use their phones during the phone charging process (i.e., chatting with their friends), resulting in an unneglectable issue of privacy leakage in practice.

10.1038/s41598-025-25174-2.title: Smartphone habits are stronger in spaces chosen out of habit
10.1038/s41598-025-25174-2.publisher: Springer Science and Business Media LLC
10.1038/s41598-025-25174-2.abstract: Habits are a fundamental driver of human behavior. However, prior research has largely studied habits in isolation. This study investigates spatial and smartphone habits-two cornerstone habits in contemporary society-in tandem, revealing the multiplicative ways in which habitual processes can underlie human behavior. We provide evidence that smartphone habits are stronger in habitually traveled and visited spaces. We leverage a custom-designed app that logged mobility and app use while prompting map-based questionnaires of participants' trips for two weeks (N = 419 participants; n = 27,446,977 GPS and app logs; n = 7,226 trip questionnaires). Overall, app habit strength predicted higher likelihood of app use (vs. non-use) and more app use (when used) on more habitually travelled routes and in more habitually visited destinations. The interaction between spatial and smartphone habits was most consistent for objective (vs. subjective) habit indicators. Social app habits were stronger and more context-independent than non-social app habits. Altogether, our findings reveal how distinct habitual processes can combine to shape daily behavior. Further, our method illustrates the potential of linking mobile sensing and surveying to measure habits in the real world.

10.1109/ichi61247.2024.00061.title: Understanding Digital Wellbeing Through Smartphone Usage Intentions and Regrettable Patterns
10.1109/ichi61247.2024.00061.abstract: The ubiquitous presence of smartphones raises significant concerns regarding their impact on users' digital well-being. This study delves into the complex relationship between smartphone usage patterns and digital wellbeing while focusing on the physical, intellectual, mental and social dimensions of well-being. We examine the smartphone usage behaviors of university students over a span of two weeks, by logging smartphone usage data and simultaneously sampling users' perceived experiences. Drawing upon data from 48 university students, we reveal that digital wellbeing is intricately connected to the how, when, and why of smartphone usage. App-switching behavior and the diversity of individualized usage patterns play pivotal roles in shaping the digital wellbeing landscape. Additionally, the promotion of intentionality in smartphone interactions emerges as a critical strategy for enhancing digital wellbeing. These findings underscore the need for personalized interventions to enhance digital wellbeing in an era dominated by digital technology.

10.1109/besc59560.2023.10386530.title: Intentions Matter: The Role of Purpose in Excessive Social Media Usage and Smartphone Addiction
10.1109/besc59560.2023.10386530.abstract: Social media offer a wide range of advantages, but excessive usage can result in negative, psychological and behavioral consequences, including addiction. This study aims to explore the role of intentions of excessive social media usage in smartphone addiction. The analysis included data from 483 respondents who answered an online survey. The results showed that intentions of excessive social media usage can be grouped into four categories: social motivations, mode modification, productive reasons, and peer pressure. The results of multiple linear regression analysis revealed that the using social media excessively for mode modification and for peer pressure were significant predictors of smartphone addiction. Additionally, multivariate analysis of variance (MANOVA) indicated significant age groups differences in intentions of excessive social media usage for peer pressure and for productive reason. Emerging adults used social media excessively for peer pressure and productive reasons compared to adults. Conversely, a significant gender-related differences in intentions of excessive social media usage for mode modification and for social motivation. Females used social media excessively for mode modification and for social motivation compared to male. These findings enhance our comprehension of the factors impacting smartphone addiction and emphasize the role of intentions of excessive social media usage in its development, overcoming the limitation of the literature on screentime, which has paid limited focus to the purpose of use.

categories.Social Media and Messaging Usage: Studies examining the role of social networking and instant messaging applications, including their usage patterns, network size, and social influences, as predictors of smartphone addiction.
categories.Contextual and Spatial Influences: Research exploring how user location, habitual travel routes, and end-user contexts (e.g., home vs. office) shape smartphone usage patterns and behaviors.
categories.Behavioral Intentions and Motivations: Investigations into the psychological and social drivers of smartphone addiction, such as social motivations, peer pressure, and mode modification, as well as demographic differences in these intentions.
categories.Digital Wellbeing and Usage Patterns: Analyses of how smartphone usage behaviors, app-switching, and intentionality impact users' physical, mental, and social well-being.
categories.Implicit Behavioral Indicators: Studies leveraging smartphone sensors, gestures, and implicit feedback mechanisms (e.g., scrolling, pinch-to-zoom) to infer user intent and engagement.
categories.Security and Privacy Concerns: Research addressing risks associated with smartphone usage, such as social engineering, VPN awareness, charging attacks, and privacy leakage during specific activities.
categories.Personalization and Adaptation: Studies focusing on the need for personalized mobile services and applications that adapt to diverse user behaviors and contexts.
categories.Barriers to Smartphone Adoption: Investigations into factors limiting smartphone usage among specific populations, such as the elderly, including financial, physical, and knowledge-related barriers.

categories.Social Media and Messaging Usage: Research on social media and messaging usage addresses the growing concern of smartphone addiction, particularly as it relates to excessive engagement with social networking applications. This body of work spans diverse use cases, including social motivations, coping with restrictions, and demographic-specific barriers. Studies like "Smartphone usage pattern based on geographical location and addiction issue" (2017) and "Intentions Matter: The Role of Purpose in Excessive Social Media Usage and Smartphone Addiction" (2024) emphasize the dominance of a few applications, such as WeChat, TikTok, and Instagram, in driving prolonged usage, with the latter highlighting the role of intentions like peer pressure and productive reasons in addiction. Methods range from behavioral analysis of app usage patterns to psychometric tools like the Smartphone Application-Based Addiction Scale (SABAS), as seen in the 2023 study linking excessive social media use to addiction and depressive symptoms. Divergences emerge in focus, with some studies, such as the 2018 investigation into VPN usage during a social media ban in Sri Lanka, exploring circumvention behaviors rather than addiction. Over time, the research has evolved from descriptive analyses of app usage (2015, 2017) to more nuanced explorations of psychological and contextual factors (2023, 2024). Outlier studies, such as the 2015 work on elderly smartphone users, reveal underexplored barriers to social media adoption, contrasting with the dominant focus on addiction. The 2023 SABAS study introduced a novel scale for quantifying addiction symptoms, while the 2018 VPN study challenged the assumption that access restrictions reduce social media engagement. Despite these advancements, gaps remain in understanding long-term behavioral shifts and the interplay between social media usage and broader societal factors, such as digital literacy and regulatory policies.
categories.Contextual and Spatial Influences: Research on contextual and spatial influences addresses the critical gap of understanding how external factors like location, time, and environment shape human behavior, particularly in the realm of smartphone usage. This category has significant applications in areas such as behavioral analytics, user authentication, and digital addiction studies. The 2017 study on smartphone usage patterns introduced the LUHA framework, demonstrating that geographical location is a dominant factor influencing app usage habits, with users' behaviors heavily tied to their physical environments. In contrast, the 2021 paper expanded the contextual lens by incorporating temporal factors, showing that patterns of app usage across specific times and days can serve as reliable inputs for behavioral authentication systems. Both studies rely on data-driven methods, with the former emphasizing location-based behavioral clustering and the latter employing learning classifier systems to model user patterns. While the LUHA framework focuses on spatial determinants, the 2021 study highlights temporal regularities, showcasing a divergence in the contextual dimensions prioritized. Over time, the field has evolved from static location-based analyses to more dynamic, multi-dimensional approaches that integrate time, location, and user-specific behaviors. Notably, the 2021 paper's use of classifier systems represents a methodological shift toward leveraging machine learning for real-time authentication, contrasting with the earlier descriptive focus of the LUHA framework. Despite these advances, the field still lacks unified models that holistically integrate spatial, temporal, and psychological factors, leaving room for future research to bridge these dimensions.
categories.Behavioral Intentions and Motivations: Research on behavioral intentions and motivations in smartphone and social media usage addresses the critical gap of understanding the psychological, social, and functional drivers behind excessive use and addiction. Key application domains include addiction studies, online learning, user authentication, and elderly technology adoption, with a focus on how user intentions shape interactions with technology. Across the studies, methods such as regression analysis, surveys, and behavioral tracking are employed to uncover patterns and predictors of usage. Notably, "Intentions Matter" (2024) identifies mode modification and peer pressure as significant predictors of smartphone addiction, with demographic nuances such as higher susceptibility among females and emerging adults. Similarly, the "Smartphone Application-Based Addiction Scale" (2023) highlights psychological factors like anxiety, depression, and sensation seeking, offering a broader personality-driven framework for understanding addiction. Diverging from these psychological and social perspectives, the "Survey on Smartphone Sensors" (2023) emphasizes behavioral indicators like scrolling and pinch-to-zoom as proxies for user intent, particularly in content consumption and online learning. In contrast, "Smartphone VPN App Usage" (2018) explores motivations rooted in distrust and perceived inevitability of data exposure, reflecting a security-conscious but resigned user mindset. The studies on elderly smartphone use (2015) and application usage patterns for authentication (2021) deviate further by focusing on functional and safety-driven motivations, with minimal attention to addiction or psychological factors. Over time, the field has evolved from examining basic functional drivers, as seen in earlier studies on elderly users, to more complex psychological and behavioral models of addiction and intent. Outlier approaches, such as using smartphone sensors for implicit intent detection, offer novel paradigms but remain underexplored in addiction contexts. While most studies align on the multifaceted nature of motivations, the "Smartphone VPN App Usage" paper challenges dominant narratives by framing user behavior as a response to external distrust rather than internal psychological drivers, highlighting the need for more nuanced, context-specific frameworks. Open gaps remain in integrating these diverse perspectives into a unified model that accounts for both psychological and functional motivations across demographic and cultural contexts.
categories.Digital Wellbeing and Usage Patterns: Research on digital wellbeing and smartphone usage patterns addresses the critical gap in understanding how excessive smartphone use impacts mental health, social relationships, and behavioral patterns, while also exploring potential interventions to mitigate these effects. Key application domains include addiction prevention, mental health monitoring, and the design of healthier digital ecosystems. Across the studies, methods range from analyzing app usage patterns and geographical influences, as seen in the 2017 study on smartphone addiction and location-based behaviors, to leveraging multimodal feedback systems for understanding user intent, as proposed in the 2023 survey on smartphone sensors. Notable contributions include the 2024 paper on the role of design features like infinite scrolling and notifications in fostering addictive behaviors, which underscores the psychological mechanisms driving excessive use. Divergences emerge in assumptions about the effects of smartphone use; for instance, while the 2023 SABAS study highlights the association between smartphone addiction and mental health issues like depression and anxiety, it also notes the potential benefits of active social app use in reducing depressive symptoms, challenging the uniformly negative narrative. Over time, the field has evolved from descriptive analyses of usage patterns to more sophisticated, sensor-driven approaches for understanding user intent and engagement. An outlier in this category is the 2021 study on user authentication through application usage patterns, which, while not directly addressing wellbeing, demonstrates the versatility of usage data for security applications. The 2023 SABAS study also introduces a nuanced paradigm by suggesting that not all smartphone use is detrimental, emphasizing the importance of context and purpose. However, the field still faces open gaps, particularly in developing scalable interventions and balancing the dual nature of smartphones as both a source of harm and a tool for social and mental health benefits. These contradictions highlight the need for more granular, context-aware research to inform design and policy.
categories.Implicit Behavioral Indicators: Research on implicit behavioral indicators addresses the challenge of unobtrusively understanding user intent, engagement, and identity through passive data collection, filling a critical gap in user experience optimization, addiction mitigation, and security. Application domains span smartphone addiction studies, social media behavior analysis, and continuous user authentication. Across the papers, smartphone sensors such as accelerometers, gyroscopes, and GPS are frequently leveraged to capture implicit behaviors like scrolling, pinch-to-zoom, and app usage patterns. Notable contributions include the 2023 survey on smartphone sensors, which comprehensively links sensor data to specific gestures and intent, and the 2021 study on behavioral authentication, which employs a Learning Classifier System to adaptively authenticate users based on app usage patterns. Divergences arise in focus areas: while the 2017 study emphasizes environmental and contextual data like location and luminosity, the 2024 paper critiques design features like infinite scrolling and notifications for their role in fostering addiction, without delving into sensor-based intent inference. Over time, the field has evolved from basic sensor data collection to sophisticated, real-time inference systems that integrate behavioral patterns into applications like security and addiction prevention. Outlier approaches include the 2021 paper’s focus on authentication, which uniquely applies implicit indicators for security rather than engagement or addiction analysis. The 2023 survey stands out for introducing a paradigm shift by explicitly linking sensor data to nuanced gestures and intent, while the 2024 study challenges dominant sensor-based approaches by emphasizing the psychological impact of design features over physical interactions. Open gaps remain in integrating these methods into holistic frameworks that address both behavioral and contextual factors, as well as reconciling the ethical implications of passive data collection with user privacy.
categories.Security and Privacy Concerns: Research on security and privacy concerns in smartphone usage highlights significant gaps in user awareness, particularly regarding the risks associated with commonly used tools like VPN apps. A 2018 study revealed that 65.15% of VPN users were unaware of potential risks, despite the fact that 75% of analyzed VPN apps utilized third-party tracking libraries and 82% requested access to sensitive information, such as user accounts and text messages. This underscores a critical need for better user education and stricter app permissions. Beyond VPNs, recent advancements in smartphone security have focused on leveraging device sensors for implicit authentication, as seen in a 2023 study that applied One-Class SVM classifiers to sensor data for enhancing PIN authentication and detecting unauthorized access. Similarly, a 2021 paper introduced a local on-device behavioral authentication model using learning classifier systems (LCS), which keeps sensitive data on the device to mitigate risks of data breaches and enhance user trust. These methods contrast with earlier studies, such as a 2015 exploration of smartphone usage among the elderly, which emphasized the perceived safety benefits of mobile phones without addressing specific security or privacy concerns. Divergences across studies include assumptions about secure communication channels, the extent of data localization, and the types of classifiers used for authentication. While most research focuses on incremental improvements to existing paradigms, the 2021 LCS-based approach represents a paradigm shift by decentralizing data processing. However, the field still lacks consensus on addressing third-party tracking and excessive permissions, as highlighted by the 2018 VPN study. Outlier studies, such as those focusing solely on user sentiment or emergency use cases, provide valuable context but do not directly tackle the core security challenges. Open gaps remain in creating universally secure frameworks that balance usability, privacy, and user education.
categories.Personalization and Adaptation: Research in personalization and adaptation seeks to address the challenge of tailoring systems to individual user needs and behaviors, particularly as these behaviors evolve over time. A prominent application domain is smartphone-based systems, where personalization can enhance user experiences in areas such as context-aware applications, user intent recognition, and behavioral authentication. Methods in this field often leverage smartphone sensors, implicit feedback, and machine learning techniques to infer user intent or adapt to behavioral changes. For instance, the 2023 study on smartphone sensors highlights the potential of sensor data to predict user intent, enabling systems like UserIntent to dynamically launch relevant applications based on inferred goals. Meanwhile, the 2021 paper on behavioral authentication employs a Learning Classifier System (LCS) with genetic algorithms and organizational learning parameters to adapt to users' shifting application usage patterns, ensuring that outdated behavioral models are replaced with up-to-date ones. Divergences across these works include the focus on intent prediction versus authentication, as well as the types of data and algorithms employed—sensor readings versus application usage patterns, and heuristic-based learning versus evolutionary algorithms. Over time, methods have evolved from static personalization approaches to adaptive systems capable of real-time updates, reflecting a broader trend toward dynamic and context-sensitive personalization. Notably, the LCS model stands out for its use of genetic algorithms to address the temporal nature of user behavior, while the UserIntent system emphasizes the integration of multimodal sensor data for immediate context recognition. Despite these advances, gaps remain in creating systems that can generalize across diverse user populations while maintaining high personalization accuracy. No papers in this set explicitly challenge the dominant adaptive paradigm, but future work may need to address trade-offs between privacy, computational efficiency, and the granularity of personalization.
categories.Barriers to Smartphone Adoption: Research on barriers to smartphone adoption addresses the critical gap of understanding why certain populations, particularly the elderly, remain resistant to integrating smartphones into their daily lives despite their ubiquity and utility. The primary application domain for this research lies in improving accessibility and inclusivity in technology design, with a focus on addressing the needs of older adults. A notable study from 2015 identifies key barriers such as financial constraints, physical limitations like vision impairments, and a lack of digital literacy, compounded by perceptions of smartphones as overly complex and unnecessary. This study highlights the emotional and practical challenges faced by elderly users, such as the fear of damaging expensive devices or difficulty using touchscreen interfaces compared to simpler "old phones." While this paper focuses on qualitative insights, other works in this category, such as those employing learning classifier systems for user authentication, explore technical solutions to enhance usability and security, though they do not directly address adoption barriers. Divergences across studies often stem from differing target demographics, with some focusing on elderly users and others on broader populations, as well as varying emphases on technical versus sociocultural factors. Over time, research in this field has evolved from merely identifying barriers to proposing targeted interventions, such as simplified interfaces or cost-effective device designs, though gaps remain in bridging these solutions to real-world adoption. Outlier approaches, such as leveraging application usage patterns for authentication, suggest innovative ways to enhance user experience but may overlook foundational adoption challenges. The 2015 study stands out for its in-depth exploration of user perceptions, offering a stark contrast to more technology-driven approaches, and underscores the need for holistic strategies that integrate both technical and human-centered solutions.
